coredata.dc:title:
Dramatic enhancement effect of carbon quantum dots on the chemiluminescence of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) reaction and application to the determination of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Hallaj, Tooba
coredata.dc:description:
We studied the effect of glucose-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs), prepared by a facile microwave method, on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) chemiluminescence (CL) reaction for the first time. It was found that the prepared CQDs enhance the CL intensity of this reaction by a factor of ~70. Possible mechanisms that lead to the effect were elucidated by recording the fluorescence and CL spectra. It was suggested that the phenomenon is probably due to the action of CQDs as reductive intermediates and also as accelerators of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
and Ce(IV) reaction. It was also shown that 4-nitrophenol at even trace levels exerts a significant increasing effect on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs CL system. This was exploited to design a new CL sensor for selective determination of 4-nirteophenol in the concentration range from 0.1 to 2.5μM, with a limit of detection of 0.03µM. 4-Nitrophenol was determined by this method in water and wastewater samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Chemiluminescence
Ru(bpy)2+ 3
4-Nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhancement of CL intensity by CQDs
Effect of mononitrophenols on the CL reaction
Optimized conditions for the CL determination of 4-nitrophenol

R

)

–Ce(IV), (b) CQDs-Ce(IV) and (c)

)

–Ce(IV)-CQDs CL reactions. Conditions:
R
u
(

)
3

, 3×10−5 M, Ce(IV), 5×10−4 M, H2SO4, 0.01M, CQDs, 50µL.

(a) CL spectrum of
R
u
(
b

y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs system, obtained with continuous flow of reagents: CQD solution and
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
(6×10−5 M) in one line and Ce(IV) (10−3 M) and H2SO4 (0.02M) in other line, (b) fluorescence spectrum of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
, at λ ex=470nm.

Effect of nitrophenol isomers on the
R
u
(

p

)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs and
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) [inset] CL systems.
Comparison of the developed method for the determination 4-nitrophenol with some previously published methods.

R
u
(
b
p

)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)- CQDs CL system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of 4-nitrophenol in real samples.

head.ce:title:
Dramatic enhancement effect of carbon quantum dots on the chemiluminescence of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) reaction and application to the determination of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We studied the effect of glucose-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs), prepared by a facile microwave method, on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) chemiluminescence (CL) reaction for the first time. It was found that the prepared CQDs enhance the CL intensity of this reaction by a factor of ~70. Possible mechanisms that lead to the effect were elucidated by recording the fluorescence and CL spectra. It was suggested that the phenomenon is probably due to the action of CQDs as reductive intermediates and also as accelerators of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
and Ce(IV) reaction. It was also shown that 4-nitrophenol at even trace levels exerts a significant increasing effect on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs CL system. This was exploited to design a new CL sensor for selective determination of 4-nirteophenol in the concentration range from 0.1 to 2.5μM, with a limit of detection of 0.03µM. 4-Nitrophenol was determined by this method in water and wastewater samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol is one of the toxic derivatives of phenol which is included in the list of priority pollutants of the USA Environmental Protection Agency. 4-Nitrophenol is used mainly to manufacture drugs, fungicides and dyes and to darken leather [1]. Thus, this compound, which is harmful to human health, animals and plants, is widely distributed into the environment via various industrial wastes, and it is difficult to degrade due to high stability. Therefore, monitoring of 4-nitrophenol has appeared of great importance in environmental analysis. Various analytical methods including chemiluminescence (CL) [2–4] and fluorescence [5,6] have been reported for this purpose.

CL, which is the emission of light from an excited species produced by a chemical reaction, is one of the powerful tools in environmental analysis, duo to several advantages such as high sensitivity, simplicity of instrumentation, low detection limits, large calibration ranges and short analysis time [7]. In the past decade, in order to improve the inherent sensitivity and expand the analytical applications of CL reactions, nanoparticles-based CL systems have been greatly developed [8–10]. One of the interesting traditional CL reagent, which has been successfully employed for the determination of various analytes, is
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
[11]. Recently the enhanced electrogenerated chemiluminescence of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
by a variety of nanoparticles have been reported. Liu et al. [12] found that AuNPs enhance the electrochemiluminescence intensity of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
which can be applied for determination of pentoxyverine. Taokaenchan et al. [13] reported an approach to enhance the electrogenerated chemiluminescence of the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
system using resonance energy transfer with CdTe in aqueous solution for the determination of nitrofuran antibiotics. Pang et al. [14] demonstrated that carbon nanodots act as the coreactants for the anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)2+ 3. Also Xu et al. [15] reported the enhancing effect of carbon dots on the electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)2+ 3 and developed a novel probe for detection of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
species. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are only one report on the study of the effect nanomaterials on the CL reaction of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
. Barnett et al. [16] have investigated the enhancing effect of AgNPs on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
CL which can be exploited for the determination of citrate ions. Compared to electrochemiluminescence methods which require extra instrumentation and suffer from electrode fouling,
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
based CL methods are simpler and faster.

Luminescent carbon nanoparticles are a new class of nanomaterials which has been employed in CL reactions in recent years. They could participate in a CL reaction as emitting species, after direct oxidation [17–22]; as catalysts of a reaction involving others luminophores [23,24]; or as emitter, after CL energy transfer [21,22,24–26]. A fascinating class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, which have attracted tremendous attention nowadays, is carbon quantum dots (CQDs). They are carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10nm which exhibit numerous unique physicochemical properties such as size- and excitation wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, resistance to photobleaching and ease of bioconjugation. Furthermore, CQDs are the eco-friendly alternatives to toxic semiconductor QDs because of low toxicity, excellent solubility, chemical inertia and stable photoluminescence [27,28]. These exceptional characteristics of CQDs make them promising in various fields such as in bioimaging [29,30], catalysis [31,32], fluorescence sensing [15,33,34], electrochemiluminescence [14,15] and chemiluminescence [20,21,35].

Here in, we investigated the effect of glucose-derived CQDs on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) CL reaction for the first time. It was found that CQDs could enhance the weak CL intensity of this reaction by about 70-fold. Moreover, based on the enhancing effect of 4-nitrophenol on the CL intensity of this system, a new CL method has been developed for the determination of trace amount of 4-nitrophenol in water samples.

The CL signals were monitored by LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). The luminometer was equipped with an automatic injector functioning on the basis of JET injection technology® and being used for injecting the oxidant. CL spectrum was recorded by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan) using flow mode with the excitation light source being turned off. The fluorescence spectra were also recorded by the same instrument under normal conditions. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com). The size and shape of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, EM 900, Zeiss, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by a Siemens D 500 instrument (Germany).

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co. Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment.
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Glucose, cerium(IV) sulfate, sulfuric acid and nitrophenol isomers were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

CQDs were synthesized from glucose by a facile microwave-assisted method according to the literature [36]. Briefly, an aqueous solution of glucose with concentration of 11wt% was prepared as carbon source. 6.0mL of this solution was put into a glass bottle with a cover, and heated with a conventional microwave oven at 900W for 10min. In the process of microwave heating, the color of the residue changes from colorless to yellow. This color change implies the formation of CQDs. The volume of obtained CQDs was reached to 6.0mL with deionized water. This solution was stable for more than one month in the refrigerator at 4°C.

CL analyses were carried out in a 3mL tube, in the batch condition with the automatic injection system, which provides high speed and repeatable mixing of the reagents. An amount of 300μL of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
(10−4 M) and 50μL of the prepared CQDs were added into the cell. Then an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution of 4-nitrophenol was added and the final volume was reached to 900μL with distilled water. After injection of 50μL of 0.01M Ce(IV) by the automatic injector, monitoring of CL signal versus time was started automatically. Maximum CL intensity was used as the analytical signal.

Well and river water samples were obtained from environs of Tabriz (Iran). Wastewater sample was obtained from a Leather Industries Area (Tabriz, Iran). In order to remove particulate materials, the wastewater was filtered with a 0.45µm filter before analysis. No pretreatment was necessary for water samples. Then, the appropriate volume of real samples was taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

In this work, a simple microwave-assisted method was used for synthesis of CQDs from glucose as a carbon source. During the microwave heating, glucose molecules are pyrolyzed and then converted to CQDs. The TEM image of prepared CQDs is shown in Fig. 1a, which indicates that they are of spherical shape and monodisperse NPs with size distribution around 10nm. The XRD pattern of CQDs was also obtained. As shown in Fig. 1b, the XRD pattern exhibits a broad (002) peak around 19° (0.46nm), which is typical of carbon nanoparticles. Fig. 1c shows the FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CQDs. The peak at 1643cm−1 is assigned to C=C stretching vibration. C=C has been formed under the microwave process duo to the glucose dehydration. Moreover, the C–O band can be observed at 1075cm−1, whereas the peaks at about 1363 and 2893cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. Additionally, the peak at about 1710cm−1 is ascribed to C=O. The broad intense peak at 3402cm−1 corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs ( Fig. 2a) exhibits two distinct absorption peaks at ~226 and ~281nm, which were attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C and the n–π* transition of C=O, respectively. Also, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2b. As can be seen, the CQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent luminescent behavior, which revealed a distribution of the various surface-defect states near the Fermi level of the CQDs. Also, the maximum fluorescence emission (~490nm) was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400nm which indicated that the maximum concentration of defects belongs to this emission state [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of CL intensity by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
We investigated the enhancement effect of CQDs on the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) CL reaction. Our studies indicated that a great enhancement (about 70-fold) in CL occurred when a small volume of CQDs solution was added to this reaction ( Fig. 3). A control experiment with the reagents used for the preparation of CQDs (the glucose solution) was carried out but this solution has no contribution to the enhancement effect. Furthermore, direct reaction of Ce(IV) with CQDs was examined. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, a weak CL signal is produced by this reaction but in the presence of both Ce(IV) and
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
, the CL intensity is much more remarkable. The kinetic profiles of CL reaction in the absence and presence of CQDs (Fig. 3) showed that the CL reaction was fast and the CL intensity reached a maximum value within 2s after injection of the oxidant solution.

In order to identify the emitting species in the CL reaction, the CL emission spectrum of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs system was recorded using a spectrofluorimeter in flow mode. The obtained CL spectrum ( Fig. 4a) has an emission band in the range of 500–700nm with a maximum located at ~600nm. As shown in Fig. 4b, this CL spectrum is similar to the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
fluorescence spectrum. Therefore, the CL may be reasonably attributed to the excited
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
.

The
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) reaction is one of the most interesting CL reactions, which has widely been studied. In this reaction,
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
is firstly oxidized to
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
, then with the aid of a reductant,
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
turned into the active compound
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
, which then return to its ground state with production of CL emission in the range of 500–700nm.

CQDs may function as reductive intermediates to reduce the generated
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
to the excited state
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
. Therefore, the quantity of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
can be greatly increased, which caused a dramatic enhancement in CL intensity. To confirm this function of CQDs, the cyclic voltammograms of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
in the presence and absence of CQDs were recorded. As seen in the Fig. S2 ( curve a, ESM) there is a pair of reversible redox peaks in the absence of CQDs. Upon addition of CQDs, the anodic peak current dramatically increased while the cathodic current tended to decrease (curve b). Indeed at the surface of electrode, CQDs can chemically reduce electrogenerated
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
to
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
which would subsequently be electro-oxidized to
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
. As a result of this fact, the oxidation peak of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
is enhanced while the reduction peak of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
is weakened. These results approve the role of CQDs as reductive intermediates [14].

In addition, as regards the negative surface charge of CQDs, it is reasonable to assume an electrostatic interaction between the CQDs and
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
. This leads to an increase in the local concentration of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
, which facilitates the interaction between
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
and Ce(IV). As a consequence, the CL reaction rate would be accelerated. Furthermore, the binding of negatively charged CQDs and the positively charged
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
may decrease the ligand π–π* energy gap and since the excitation and emission in the CL reaction were due to charge transfer transitions between a d-orbital on the ruthenium and a π* anti-bonding orbital on the ligand, it can increase the number of excited state molecules (
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
), which leads to the enhancement of the photon efficiency [12,16].
Based on the above discussion, the enhanced CL mechanism can be summarized as follows:

R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
+Ce(IV)
→
CQDs
Ce(III)+Ru(bpy)3 3+

R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
+CQDs→Ru(bpy)3 2+*

R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
→Ru(bpy)3 2++hυ (~600nm)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mononitrophenols on the CL reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to assess the analytical applicability of the
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs CL system, the effect of nitrophenol isomers on this reaction and also on
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) system itself was examined. The obtained results ( Fig. 5) indicated that nitrophenols could enhance the CL intensity of both systems and that para isomer had more remarkable enhancing effect than other two isomers. In nitrophenols, hydroxy substituent acts as an electron donor and nitro substituent as an electron acceptor. So, it can be supposed that nitrophenols function as reductants to reduce the generated
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
3 +
to the excited state
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 + *
, which leads to an improvement in the CL emission intensity. At the same time, the hydroxy group of nitrophenol would be oxidized to a radical [37]. When nitro substituent is present at para position, its resonance effect is more pronounced and so the radical would be more stabilized. Hence, the para isomer of nitrophenol exerts more remarkable effect on the CL intensity. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 5 that the selectivity factor for 4-nitrophenol is much greater in the presence of CQDs than its absence. Also the magnitude of response (ΔI) and, hence the sensitivity, is much larger using
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)-CQDs reaction. Finally, the CL spectra in the absence and presence of nitrophenols (Fig. S3, ESM) show that the emitting species is the same in all cases. Based on this phenomena, we designed a new selective CL sensor for the determination of 4-nirteophenol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized conditions for the CL determination of 4-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for the determination of 4-nitrophenol, the reaction conditions were optimized in terms of concentration of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
, Ce(IV), H2SO4 and CQDs. The respective data are given in the Electronic Supporting Material (Fig. S1). The optimized conditions are (1) 3.0×10−5 molL−1
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
, (2) 5.0×10−4 molL−1 Ce(IV), (3) 0.01molL−1 sulfuric acid and (4) 50μL of CQD solution.

Based on the enhanced CL reaction, a sensitive method was developed for the determination of 4-nitrophenol. Under the optimum conditions described above, the analytical figures of merit for determination of 4-nitrophenol was obtained. The CL response was found to be linear in the concentration range from 0.1 to 2.5µM with a limit of detection (3s) of 0.03µM. The regression equation was ΔI= 238,159C+ 75,748, R ²=0.9964, where ΔI=I–I 0 is the difference between the CL intensity in the presence of 4-nitrophenol (I) and in its absence (I 0), and C is concentration of 4-nitrophenol in µM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained to be 2.1% for five replicate determinations of 1.0µM 4-nitrophenol. The results indicate that this CL system has a good linearity, relatively high sensitivity and suitable precision. Comparison between the developed method and some other reported CL and fluorescence methods for the 4-nitrophenol quantification is shown in Table 1. As can be seen, the developed method has better limit of detection than most of other methods. Moreover, our method is much simpler than the methods involving liquid chromatography [3] and fluorescence [5] in terms of instrumentation. Compared to other reported CL method [2], which uses molecularly imprinted polymer-capped QDs, our method is simpler and faster because the synthesis procedure of CQDs is much simpler and less time-consuming than that of MIP-capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs.

The interfering effect of some species commonly found in environmental water samples, on the determination of 1.0µM 4-nitrophenol was investigated. The tolerable concentrations for interfering species in relative error of <5% were summarized in Table 2. These results indicate that the method possesses a good selectivity for the determination of 4-nitrophenol in real samples.

The developed method was applied to the determination of 4-nitrophenol in real water and wastewater samples. In order to confirm the accuracy of the method, known quantities of 4-nitrophenol were added to the samples and then they were prepared and analyzed according to the general procedure. The obtained results are shown in Table 3. Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test showed that there are no significant differences between the added and found values.

It was demonstrated that glucose-derived CQDs, prepared by microwave-assisted method, greatly enhance the CL intensity of
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV) reaction. This effect was attributed to the possible action of CQDs as reductive intermediates and also as electron transfer accelerators. Additionally, it was found that in the presence of trace levels of 4-nitrophenol, the CL intensity of this system increased. This was used to develop a novel CL method for the determination of 4-nitrophenol with high selectivity and sensitivity. The established method has been applied to the determination of 4-nitrophenol in water and wastewater samples with satisfactory results. Finally, it should be mentioned that
R
u
(
b
p
y
)
3
2 +
–Ce(IV)- CQDs, as a novel CL system, may have other potential analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 93024484).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2015.11.019.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, Toxicological profile for nitrophenol (1992). 〈http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/toxprofiles/tp50.pdf〉

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta. A. Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
